title: The return of creativity after trauma suppressed it
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

htmlContent:
The moment I knew I'd lost something

I was sitting on my bedroom floor, a sketchbook open in my lap. Blank. I'd been staring at that blank page for forty minutes. My hand wouldn't move. Not because I didn't want to draw, but because something in me had locked up tight. I remember feeling this hollow ache in my chest, like I was grieving someone I used to be.

That someone was the kid who used to fill whole notebooks with drawings. Who wrote stories on napkins. Who built elaborate worlds out of cardboard boxes and imagination. That kid disappeared somewhere along the way. And I didn't even notice until years later.

Here's the thing. Trauma doesn't just steal your sense of safety. It doesn't just mess with your relationships or your sleep or your ability to trust. It also steals your creativity. Quietly. Without you even realizing it's happening.

You might think, "I'm just not creative anymore." Or, "I grew out of it." Or, "I was never really talented anyway." But what if that's not true? What if your creativity isn't gone, just buried under layers of survival?

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma freezes our bodies. We get stuck in fight, flight, or freeze. And creativity, real creativity, requires a body that feels safe enough to play. You can't play when you're scanning for threats. You can't imagine new possibilities when your nervous system is convinced the world is dangerous.

I want to tell you something that might sting a little. The part of you that stopped making things, that part wasn't broken. That part was smart. That part knew that when you're in survival mode, creativity is a luxury you can't afford. Because creativity requires vulnerability. It requires uncertainty. It requires letting yourself be seen.

And when you've been hurt, those things feel terrifying.

What actually happens to the creative drive

Let's get specific about the mechanism here. Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," has spent decades studying how trauma reshapes the brain. One of the things he found is that trauma actually changes the way your brain processes information. The left prefrontal cortex, the part that helps you plan, imagine, and create, goes offline when you're in a threat response.

Your brain reroutes resources to the parts that keep you alive. The amygdala. The brainstem. The parts that scan for danger and prepare your body to fight or run. Creativity is not a survival priority. It's a luxury that only shows up when you feel safe.

So if you've been living in a state of chronic hypervigilance, which is incredibly common with complex trauma, your brain has been actively suppressing your creative drive for years. Maybe decades. Not because you're not creative, but because your nervous system decided that creativity wasn't safe.

Think about what creativity actually requires. You have to tolerate not knowing how something will turn out. You have to be okay with making mistakes. You have to let yourself be imperfect in public. You have to access emotions that might feel overwhelming. For a trauma survivor, that's a minefield.

I remember trying to write poetry again after a particularly bad period of flashbacks. I'd get maybe three lines in, and then this wave of shame would wash over me. "This is stupid. Nobody wants to read this. You're just being dramatic." That voice wasn't my inner critic. That voice was my survival system trying to protect me from the vulnerability of being seen.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker breaks down exactly how the inner critic operates and how it's actually a trauma response, not a reflection of who you really are. Reading that book helped me understand that my creative blocks weren't personal failures. They were symptoms of a nervous system that had been trying to keep me safe.

The slow, awkward return

Here's what nobody tells you about reclaiming creativity after trauma. It's not going to feel good at first. It's going to feel awkward, embarrassing, and kind of painful. That's normal. That's the part where your nervous system is learning that it's safe to create again.

When I started drawing again, my hands shook. My lines were jagged and uncertain. I'd erase things ten times before I was willing to commit to a mark. It looked like a child's drawing, and I'm not being self-deprecating. It genuinely looked like a five-year-old had done it. And I had to let that be okay.

Janina Fisher, who specializes in treating trauma and dissociation, talks about how our traumatized parts need to be approached with curiosity, not force. You can't bully yourself into being creative. You can't shame yourself into making art. That just reinforces the message that creativity is dangerous.

Instead, you have to start small. Ridiculously small. Like, "I will draw one line on this paper today" small. Or "I will write three sentences and then close the notebook" small. The goal isn't to produce something good. The goal is to teach your nervous system that creativity doesn't mean death.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz. His Internal Family Systems model helped me understand that the part of me that wanted to create and the part of me that was terrified of creating weren't enemies. They were both trying to protect me in different ways.

Stay with me here. This part is important.

The return of creativity isn't linear. You'll have days where the words flow and the colors blend and everything feels alive. And then you'll have weeks where the blank page stares back at you and you feel like a fraud. That's not regression. That's your nervous system checking in, making sure it's still safe. It's like a scared animal that keeps running back to its hiding spot to see if the coast is clear.

You don't need to force it. You just need to keep showing up.

What actually helps the creative self come back

I'm going to be direct with you here. The wellness industry will tell you to "just start creating" or "push through the resistance." That's toxic positivity dressed up as advice. It doesn't work for trauma survivors because it ignores the biological reality of what's happening in your nervous system.

What actually helps is creating safety in your body first. Before you ever pick up a pen or a paintbrush or a musical instrument, you need to signal to your nervous system that you're not in danger. That might mean spending five minutes just breathing and noticing where your body feels tight. That might mean wrapping yourself in a blanket. That might mean sitting in a specific corner of your room that feels protected.

Deb Dana, who studies polyvagal theory and its application to trauma recovery, emphasizes that we need to find our "glimmers" first. Glimmers are small moments of safety and connection. Before you can create, you need to find a glimmer. A warm cup of tea. The feeling of sun on your skin. The sound of rain on the roof. Something that tells your body, "We're okay right now."

Then, and only then, can you try to create. And even then, it might not work. That's okay.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) by Lindsay C. Gibson. For me, understanding how my creativity was suppressed in childhood helped me stop blaming myself for losing it. It wasn't my fault. I was adapting to an environment where being seen was dangerous.

I also want to mention something about the relationship between creativity and shame. Brené Brown isn't a trauma researcher per se, but her work on vulnerability and shame is incredibly relevant here. She says that shame is the fear of disconnection. "If people see my art, my writing, my music, they'll see how flawed I am, and they'll reject me." That fear is real. And it's rooted in real experiences of being rejected or hurt when you were vulnerable.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach. Her work helped me stop fighting the shame and start just letting it be there while I created anyway. You don't have to feel confident to create. You just have to be willing to feel uncomfortable and do it anyway.

What the return actually looks like

I want to be honest about what this process looks like in real life. It's not a triumphant comeback where you suddenly paint a masterpiece and everyone applauds. It's more like a tentative, awkward reacquaintance with an old friend you haven't seen in years.

You might start by just looking at art instead of making it. You might read books about creativity before you ever try to create anything yourself. You might doodle in the margins of your notebook and then immediately hide it. You might write a poem and delete it before anyone sees it. That's all part of the process.

Right? You're not failing. You're negotiating with your survival system.

I remember the first time I actually finished a piece of writing after years of creative suppression. It was a short essay about grief, maybe 800 words. I wrote it in a coffee shop, and my hands were sweating the whole time. I felt like I was doing something illegal. When I finished, I closed my laptop and cried for ten minutes. Not because it was good, but because I had done it. I had proven to myself that I still could.

And then I didn't write anything for another three weeks. That's the rhythm of it. Bursts of creative energy followed by long stretches of silence. The silence used to scare me. I thought it meant I was broken again. Now I understand that the silence is my nervous system's way of processing what just happened. It needs time to integrate the experience of being creative without being destroyed.

Notice what just happened in your body when I said that. Did you feel relief? Did you feel a tightening? Did you feel hope? Just notice. Don't try to change it.

Wild, right? How your body responds to the mere possibility of reclaiming something you thought you'd lost forever.

I'll leave you with this. The creativity that trauma suppressed isn't gone. It's not dead. It's been waiting. It's been hibernating in the parts of you that learned to stay small and quiet and safe. And those parts, they're not your enemies. They're the parts that kept you alive when you needed to survive.

But you're not just surviving anymore. You're learning to live. And living includes making things. Even if they're imperfect. Even if nobody sees them. Even if they feel clumsy and awkward and not quite right.

That's how it starts. And that's enough.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - Pete Walker's guide to understanding and healing complex trauma, including the inner critic and emotional flashbacks.
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - Richard Schwartz's introduction to Internal Family Systems therapy, which helps you understand and befriend the different parts of yourself, including the parts that block creativity.
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) - Lindsay C. Gibson's exploration of how growing up with emotionally unavailable parents affects your sense of self and your ability to express yourself.
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - Tara Brach's gentle guide to working with shame and self-rejection through mindfulness and compassion.

As an Amazon Associate, I earn from qualifying purchases.
